ocr.text: The impact of incarceration on physical functioning is a critical area of study, particularly in understanding the health outcomes
of individuals before and after imprisonment. The data presented in the table highlights significant changes in physical
functioning scores, as measured by the mean values on entry to prison and three months after imprisonment.
Women still in prison at three months,n = 92  Difference[95% confidence interval]  Significance
On entry to prisonMean (sd)  Three months after imprisonmentMean (sd)
general health perception  49.8 (25.4)  58.2 (23.0)  8.4 [3.5 to 13.2]  0.001
Mental Component Summary score  35.5 (14.7)  40.1 (14.0)  4.6 [1.7 to 7.5]  0.002
Physical Component Summary score  44.3 (12.0)  46.3 (12.2)  1.9 [-0.6 to 4.4]  0.131
Initially, individuals entering prison reported a mean physical functioning score of 72.6 (sd = 29.1), reflecting the various health
challenges they faced prior to incarceration. However, after three months of imprisonment, this score increased to 80.6 (sd =
25.5), indicating a notable improvement in physical health during the early stages of confinement. The statistical analysis
further reveals a mean difference of 8.0, with a 95% confidence interval ranging from 2.0 to 13.0, and a p-value of 0.002,
suggesting that the change is statistically significant. These findings underscore the potential influence of the prison
environment and healthcare interventions on the physical well-being of inmates, highlighting the importance of ongoing
evaluation of health programs within correctional facilities to enhance inmate health outcomes during and after their period of
incarceration.
